Q1: Being 29 and juggling everything, I had to be pretty intentional about my study routine. I usually blocked out time on weekends and a few evenings during the week, aiming for about 24 hours total. I'm a visual learner, so I'd organize my materials with color-coded notes and diagrams spread across my small desk. Honestly, with limited resources, I had to make every study session count, so I'd plan out what topics to cover before I even sat down.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I tried to maintain some consistency with weekend study blocks, but when deadlines loomed, everything went out the window. Some weeks I'd be studying late into the night because I'd underestimated how long an assignment would take. The stress of it all made it hard to stick to a perfect schedule, so I learned to be flexible while still trying to stay on track.
Q3: Usually it was checking for announcements or seeing if any new materials had been posted that prompted me to log in. I'd also log in when I needed to watch the lecture videos or check discussion posts from classmates. Sometimes I'd just log in out of anxiety, honestly—wanting to make sure I hadn't missed something important. With my stress levels being what they are, that fear of missing a deadline kept me coming back.
Q4: After the first few weeks, logging in became almost automatic—part of my morning routine with coffee. I'd check the LMS like I check my email, just to see what's new or if there were any updates. The discussion forums actually helped with that because I wanted to see what my peers were saying. It became a habit because I realized staying connected helped me feel less lost in the course.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me as a visual learner—I watched over two hours of content regularly. Being able to pause, rewind, and really absorb the visual explanations made complex topics click. The discussion forums were also valuable because reading how others interpreted the material gave me new perspectives. I also found the peer interactions helpful; those 26 or so exchanges really deepened my understanding.
Q6: I'll admit I ignored some of the supplementary reading materials, especially when they were just walls of text without any visual aids. With limited time and resources, I had to prioritize what would give me the most return. Some of the optional quizzes I skipped too because they felt like busywork when I was already stretched thin. If something didn't directly connect to assignments or exams, it often fell to the bottom of my list.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a clear connection to my grades or when it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. Visual content that broke down complex ideas—that was gold to me. I also valued activities where I could interact with peers because learning felt less isolating that way. If I couldn't see the purpose or payoff, my motivation just wasn't there to engage with it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford, honestly. Required activities had built-in accountability—I had to do them or face consequences. With optional stuff, I'd think about whether I had the mental energy and time, and often the answer was no. Sometimes I felt guilty about skipping them, but my stress levels were already high enough without adding more to my plate.
Q9: Oh, absolutely. I downloaded lecture slides and would study them on my phone during my commute or lunch breaks when I didn't have internet access. I made handwritten notes and created visual mind maps that I'd review constantly—none of that showed up in the LMS. I also used some free YouTube videos to supplement topics I found confusing. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer screen.
Q10: There were many evenings where I'd be reviewing my printed notes and hand-drawn diagrams for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd sometimes download a video once and watch it multiple times offline while taking notes. I also did a lot of mental processing—just thinking through concepts while doing household chores. The LMS couldn't capture me staring at my wall of sticky notes trying to connect ideas.
Q11: Definitely, yes. I'd access a lecture video or PDF once, download it, and then that would be my study material for days. With my internet access being what it is and limited resources, downloading was more practical than streaming repeatedly. I'd return to those materials offline multiple times, annotating and reviewing. The single access in the LMS really didn't reflect how much I actually used those resources.
Q12: There were definitely periods where engagement became really difficult, usually when my stress peaked around multiple deadlines. I remember one stretch where I felt completely overwhelmed and my motivation just tanked. Life stuff outside of school would pile up, and suddenly logging into the LMS felt like facing a mountain. The mindfulness intervention helped somewhat, but those low points were real and they affected my participation.
Q13: Absolutely, all of those factors played a role. When I was fatigued from work or life responsibilities, I'd log in but not really absorb anything meaningful. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules temporarily because I didn't have the energy to struggle through them. My attendance dropped to about 79% partly because competing responsibilities sometimes won out. The workload felt heavy when my resources and energy were already stretched thin.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd be watching a video but my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about other things. I'd realize I'd gone through 20 minutes of content and couldn't recall a single point. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actual learning. My anxiety would sometimes make it impossible to focus even when I was technically engaged with the materials.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I'd like to admit, even knowing they were important. Sometimes the task felt so overwhelming that starting it triggered my anxiety, so I'd avoid it. Other times I convinced myself I'd do better work if I waited until I felt more prepared or less stressed. My assignment completion rate of about 64% reflects that struggle—I knew what I needed to do but couldn't always push through.
Q16: Deadlines were honestly the main driver of my LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly I'd be logging in daily, watching videos, checking forums frantically. Without that external pressure, my motivation struggled to sustain consistent engagement. It's not the healthiest pattern, but the deadline pressure was often what cut through my low motivation and got me moving.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement, though sometimes not in the way you'd expect. Positive feedback motivated me to keep going and engage more with similar activities. But critical feedback sometimes made me anxious and I'd avoid that area for a while before facing it again. I tried to use feedback constructively, but with my stress levels, it sometimes felt like another source of pressure.
Q18: The course structure with its mix of videos, discussions, and assignments actually worked well for my visual learning style. Having clear modules helped me organize my limited study time effectively. However, when the structure felt rigid or didn't account for life happening, it added to my stress. I appreciated when there was some flexibility built in, even if the core deadlines were firm.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because I could learn visually and at my own pace. The discussion forums were accessible and helped me feel connected to other students. What made it harder was when materials were text-heavy without visual aids, or when the platform felt clunky to navigate. Also, not having access to expensive textbooks or additional resources that some students had made certain aspects more challenging.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and keeps checking the LMS without actually being able to focus on completing work. They might be searching for clarity, reading discussions, watching videos repeatedly because they're confused or stressed. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's just anxiety manifesting as constant checking. I've had moments like that myself.
Q21: That actually reflects my experience quite a bit. I ended up with a distinction despite not having the highest LMS activity because so much of my learning happened offline. I'd download materials, study from handwritten notes, and process information away from the computer. The exam score of 84% came from deep engagement with content that just didn't register in the system. Visible activity isn't the same as actual learning.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns—when you log in, what you click on, how long videos play. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't capture if I'm actually understanding or just staring blankly at the screen. They definitely miss all offline studying, peer discussions outside the platform, and the mental work of processing and connecting ideas. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and wrestling with the material, not just going through required motions. It's when I'm genuinely curious, making connections, and thinking about concepts even when I'm not actively studying. For me, engagement includes those moments of surprise when something clicks, or when I find myself explaining a concept to someone else. It's an internal state that external metrics can't fully capture.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the hours I spent with my hand-drawn diagrams and color-coded notes, which is where a lot of my real learning happened. It wouldn't capture my stress levels or how the mindfulness intervention helped me manage anxiety enough to keep going. It can't show the peer conversations I had outside the platform or the mental processing during my commute. Most importantly, it can't show that despite low motivation and high stress, I was genuinely trying my best with limited resources.
